items.q: What does gravitational field strength at a point represent?
items.correct: The force per unit mass that would act on a test mass placed at that point.
items.misconception: The total gravitational force at that point regardless of mass.
items.random_wrong: The mass of the object creating the field.
items.why: Students forget the 'per unit mass' (or per unit charge) part of field strength definitions, conflating the field with the resulting force.

items.q: Does Hooke's law apply to a spring for any amount of stretching?
items.correct: No — it only holds up to the limit of proportionality; beyond that the relationship between force and extension is no longer linear.
items.misconception: Yes — Hooke's law applies to any spring at any extension.
items.random_wrong: Only when the force is small enough to be ignored.
items.why: Students apply F = kx universally without noting the limit of proportionality, leading to over-prediction at large extensions.

items.q: Why do atoms emit light only at specific wavelengths rather than a continuous spectrum?
items.correct: Electrons occupy discrete energy levels; transitions between levels emit photons of specific frequencies (E = hf).
items.misconception: Atoms can emit any wavelength, but only certain ones are visible.
items.random_wrong: Atoms emit only one colour each, determined by their temperature.
items.why: Students don't internalise the quantisation of energy levels; without it, line spectra look arbitrary instead of a direct consequence of quantum mechanics.

items.q: A 2 kg object accelerates at 4 m/s². What is the resultant force?
items.why: It's 8 N. “2 N” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does it mean if the resultant force on an object is zero?
items.correct: The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.
items.why: It's The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.. “The object must be stationary.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Calculate the weight in newtons of a 5 kg mass on Earth (g = 9.8 N/kg).
items.why: The real figure is 49 N. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
